info.topictitle: 2.3.2 Shifting demand and supply curves to produce a new market equilibrium

markdown:
At **market equilibrium**, both consumers and producers are **satisfied** and have **no tendency** to change their choices, **unless** there is a change in some **non-price determinant.**

![Figure 1: initial market equilibrium](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/cf7d19dcf1f3a8234296291c4b3d9fef6cee0ef7-2338x1813.png)

Consider the market for some good $X$ (Figure 1). At first, it is in equilibrium at point $A$ with price $P_e$ and quantity $Q_e$.

### Shifts in Demand


Now assume that there is a change in some **non-price determinant**, such as an increase in the price of a substitute of good $X$:** **

1. This will cause the demand for good $X$ to **increase**, **shifting** the curve to the **right** from $D_1$ to $D_2$ (Figure 2).
2. Hence, at the current price $P_e$, there is an** excess demand** from point $B$ to $A$ because the **new quantity demanded** $Q_1$ is higher than the **quantity supplied** $Q_e$ .
3. Thus (as we was in 2.3.1), the firm will **increase its price** until $P_1$ causing a new equilibrium point to be found at $C$ with quantity $Q_2$.

![Figure 2: establishment of a new equilibrium point following a rightward shift in the demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/416c31253b54f47aba32e14407272f5269c6176d-2338x1813.png)

Similarly, now assume that there is the opposite effect through a **non-price determinant**, such as the discovery of good $X$'s harmful health effects (negative preferences and tastes):

1. This will cause the demand for good $X$ to **decrease**, **shifting** the curve to the **left** from $D_1$ to $D_2$.
2. Hence, at the current price $P_e$, there is an** excess supply (surplus)** from point $B$ to $A$ because the **new quantity demanded** $Q_1$ is lower than the **quantity supplied** $Q_e$. 
3. Thus (2.3.1), the firm will **decrease its price** until $P_1$ causing a new equilibrium point to be found at $C$ with quantity $Q_2$.

![Figure 3: establishment of a new equilibrium point following a leftward shift in the demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/63cccfe4087aa52e5c81ff284c29b982c79efc99-2338x1813.png)

### Shifts in Supply


Now, assume some change considering **non-price determinant's **again but from the **supply** side, such as a fall of the cost of raw materials:

1. This will cause the supply for good $X$ to **increase**, **shifting** the curve to the **right** from $S_1$ to $S_2$ (Figure 3).
2. Hence, at the current price $P_e$, there is an** excess supply** from point $B$ to $A$ because the **new quantity supplied** $Q_1$ is higher than the **quantity demanded** $Q_e$ .
3. Thus (2.3.1), the firm will **decrease its price **_(downward pressure)_ until $P_1$ causing a new equilibrium point to be found at $C$ with quantity $Q_2$.

![Figure 4: establishment of a new equilibrium point following a rightward shift in the supply curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7525c5078a9f9f9eab3e89c91e33234ee2dcec63-2338x1813.png)

Finally let there be the opposite change considering **non-price determinant's**, this time an increase in cost of raw materials:

1. This will cause the supply for good $X$ to **decrease**, **shifting** the curve to the **left** from $S_1$ to $S_2$ (Figure 4).
2. Hence, at the current price $P_e$, there is an** excess demand** from point $B$ to $A$ because the **new quantity supplied** $Q_1$ is lower than the **quantity demanded** $Q_e$. 
3. Thus (2.3.1), the firm will **increase its price **_(upward pressure)_ until $P_1$ causing a new equilibrium point to be found at $C$ with quantity $Q_2$.

![Figure 5: establishment of a new equilibrium point following a leftward shift in the supply curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/70d19083ff90a776cc77239b010150cc9a7447fa-2338x1813.png)

<callout type="note">Our discussion above has showcased 4 ways in which the equilibrium can change. Remember, all those shift are caused by changes in the **non-price determinants**.</callout>

<callout type="self_review">Consider the market of good $X$ in equilibrium. Explain the following using a diagram.

1. What will happen if the income rises for all consumers?
2. What will happen if a lot of firms join the market?
3. What about if some material of good $X$ turns out to be really harmful?</callout>

prevtopic.title: 2.3.1 Demand and supply curves forming a market equilibrium
